typical_length.description: long - posts are detailed and substantive, often combining project data, technical analysis, and personal commentary

post_openings.description: He typically opens with a rhetorical question, a provocative statement, a bold factual claim about a building, or a teaser/hook about upcoming podcast episodes. Short punchy sentences or fragments are common openers.
patterns: Rhetorical or curiosity question about a building (מי יציל את הבניין? / האם ידעתם ש...? / יש מצב שככה יראו המבנים בעתיד??)
Bold declarative statement about a project or issue (הבנק הגדול ביותר בטורקיה... / שלשום פרצה שריפה...)
Teaser/preview of upcoming podcast episode (בשבוע הבא עולה שיחה... / איזה פרק עולה מחר!)
Announcement of company news or partnerships (אני שמח לשתף שחתמנו...)
Personal observation or short provocative fragment (גם לכם נמאס מזה?! / דופק, טנדר וסט כלים - זה לא מספיק...)
Naming/introducing a building (הכירו את... / תכירו את...)
examples: אין מי שביקר בברצלונה ולא שם לב למבנה המודרני הזה.
המוזיאון הזה נבנה במקום שבו דובאי הכריזה על עצמאות בשנת 1971
יש בעיה בארץ… והרבה לא מכירים אותה...
המבנה הזה מחזיק בשיא גינס! יש לכם ניחוש על מה?
כשהבנין הזה נבנה ב2007 הוא הדהים את כל עולם האדריכלות
בא לכם קפה? אז שווה לכם לקפוץ למשרדים בפרויקט
בחיים לא תצליחו לנחש את הסיבה ל״שפיץ״ העצום של המגדל בתמונה
איזו חיה המבנה הזה מזכיר לכם?

sentence_structure.description: Alternates between short punchy fragments for emphasis and longer flowing explanatory sentences for technical content. Bullet-point lists appear frequently for technical specs. Uses dashes, ellipses, and line breaks liberally to create rhythm and pacing. Technical paragraphs can be long, but are often interrupted by shorter commentary sentences. Informal Hebrew contractions and colloquialisms are common.

tone.description: Primarily educational — he teaches readers about building envelope technology, global projects, materials, and industry issues with genuine expertise. Secondary tone is conversational and personal: he uses first person, shares opinions, makes jokes, addresses readers directly ('אתם', 'חברים'), and invites engagement. Occasionally inspirational when discussing values, persistence, or quality. Uses informal Hebrew register throughout, mixing professional depth with accessible language.

patterns: Direct question to readers inviting engagement or opinions
CTA to leave a comment for podcast link
Soft call to action (follow, share, contact)
examples: מה אתם חושבים? שווה לבקר שם?
אם אתם רוצים את הפרק המלא- תשאירו תגובה ואדאג לשלוח לכם אותו
מה דעתכם? הייתם רוצים לגור בבניין כזה?
תמשיכו לעקוב. אני אמשיך לעדכן בעוד פרויקטים מיוחדים.
אם זה מדבר אליך - תעשה סימן
נ.ב. אנחנו בקרוב נתחיל בעבודת תיכנון של מבנה בישראל...

hebrew_english_mixing.pattern: Posts are written almost entirely in Hebrew, but English is embedded naturally and frequently within Hebrew sentences for technical terms, brand names, project names, and professional titles. English proper nouns (architect firms, building names, standards) appear in Latin script within Hebrew text without translation. Technical acronyms (LEED, ETFE, GRC, ACP, BIM, IGU, HPL) are always written in English. When introducing people, their LinkedIn-style English name often appears alongside the Hebrew.
hebrew_english_mixing.english_usage: English is used for: (1) industry-standard technical terms that have no common Hebrew equivalent (curtain wall, unitized, thermal bridging, cold-bent glass, parametric, diagrid); (2) certification standards and ratings (LEED Platinum, BREEAM, GSAS, Fitwel); (3) proper names of architects, firms, companies, and buildings; (4) materials brand names (Alucobond, Schüco, Trespa, Fundermax); (5) English-origin professional concepts that are used verbatim in the Israeli industry (mock-up, value engineering, ROI, AI, BIM, scan-to-BIM); (6) occasional short English phrases for effect or when addressing international context.

readability.description: Written in accessible but professional Hebrew, targeting industry practitioners (architects, engineers, developers, contractors). Vocabulary is specialized but explained — he regularly defines technical terms inline (e.g., 'IGU הוא קיצור של Insulated Glass Units... בעברית המבינים שבינכם זה נקרא ״בידודית״'). Sentence length varies: short punchy sentences for impact, longer explanatory sentences for technical content. Informal register, colloquialisms, and humor lower the barrier. Lists and structured data (project specs) aid comprehension. Not written for laypeople but not inaccessible to educated non-specialists.

recurring_themes: Iconic global building envelope / facade projects — detailed analysis of cladding systems, materials, and engineering
Podcast episode teasers, releases, and guest introductions
Building safety and fire risk from facade materials (ACP with PE core, Grenfell references)
Sustainability, green building standards (LEED, BREEAM, GSAS, Fitwel, Green Star)
Israeli construction industry critique — lack of regulation, low knowledge, poor maintenance culture
Company news — partnerships, new hires, new services, events (Zak World of Façades)
Technology and innovation — CNC fabrication, parametric design, AI in construction, 3D laser scanning
Building envelope engineering deep-dives — curtain wall systems, dry cladding, double skin, cold-bent glass
Material education — ACP/Alucobond, GRC, GFRP, terracotta, titanium, ETFE, HPL
Professional values — quality over cost-cutting, importance of maintenance, early consultant involvement
Job postings for facade planning roles
things_to_avoid: Generic motivational content without connection to the building envelope industry
Overly formal or academic language — the tone is always accessible and personal
Posting without a clear hook, question, or narrative tension at the opening
Using only emojis for structure — he uses them sparingly and mostly at closings
Writing long posts without bullet points, paragraph breaks, or structural variation
Avoiding personal opinions — he always expresses a clear point of view
Missing the CTA or engagement question at the close of educational posts
Neglecting technical depth in building-related posts — superficial descriptions are out of character
Using only English — Hebrew is always the primary language even for technical posts
Skipping project credits and data (location, architects, height, year) in building showcase posts
Posting about buildings without connecting to the facade/envelope dimension
Excessive self-promotion without providing educational value
